section.title: The Prologue and the Birth of John Foretold
section.sectionRationale: Luke opens his Gospel twice over: first with a formal preface explaining why he writes, then with the scene that begins the story. A careful historian dedicates his orderly account to Theophilus so that he may know the certainty of what he has been taught. Then the curtain rises in the temple, where an aging, childless priest named Zechariah meets the angel Gabriel and hears that his prayers are answered: a son will be born who will go before the Lord in the spirit of Elijah. It is one movement, the writer's purpose and the first act of fulfillment.
section.thesis: Luke writes so that his reader may know the certainty of the gospel, and he begins that gospel where Israel's long silence breaks: in the temple, God answers an old couple's prayer and sends the forerunner who will prepare a people for the Lord.
section.frameNote: Six beats: the orderly account for Theophilus; a righteous, childless couple; Gabriel at the altar of incense; the promise of John; Zechariah's doubt and silence; and Elizabeth's hidden joy.

units.label: The orderly account for Theophilus
units.note: so that you may know the certainty

verses.text: Many have undertaken to compose an account of the things that have been fulfilled among us,
just as they were handed down to us by the initial eyewitnesses and servants of the word.
Therefore, having carefully investigated everything from the beginning, it seemed good also to me to write an {{kathexes|orderly}} account for you, most excellent Theophilus,
so that you may know the {{asphaleia|certainty}} of the things you have been taught.

units.label: A righteous, childless couple
units.note: no children, well along in years

verses.text: In the time of Herod king of Judea there was a priest named Zechariah, who belonged to the priestly division of Abijah, and whose wife Elizabeth was a descendant of Aaron.
Both of them were righteous in the sight of God, walking blamelessly in all the commandments and decrees of the Lord.
But they had no children, because Elizabeth was barren, and they were both well along in years.

units.label: Gabriel at the altar of incense

verses.text: One day while Zechariah’s division was on duty and he was serving as priest before God,
he was chosen by lot, according to the custom of the priesthood, to enter the temple of the Lord and burn incense.
And at the hour of the incense offering, the whole congregation was praying outside.
Just then an angel of the Lord appeared to Zechariah, standing at the right side of the altar of incense.
When Zechariah saw him, he was startled and gripped with fear.

units.label: The promise of John
units.note: in the spirit and power of Elijah

verses.text: But the angel said to him, “Do not be afraid, Zechariah, because your prayer has been heard. Your wife Elizabeth will bear you a son, and you are to give him the name John.
He will be a joy and delight to you, and many will rejoice at his birth,
for he will be great in the sight of the Lord. He shall never take wine or strong drink, and he will be filled with the {{pneuma|Holy Spirit}} even from his mother’s womb.
Many of the sons of Israel he will turn back to the Lord their God.

And he will go on before the Lord in the spirit and power of Elijah, to turn the hearts of the fathers to their children and the disobedient to the wisdom of the righteous, to make ready a people prepared for the Lord.”

units.label: Zechariah's doubt and silence
units.note: you will be silent until the day this comes to pass

verses.text: “How can I be sure of this?” Zechariah asked the angel. “I am an old man, and my wife is well along in years.”
“I am Gabriel,” replied the angel. “I stand in the presence of God, and I have been sent to speak to you and to bring you this {{euangelizo|good news}}.
And now you will be silent and unable to speak until the day this comes to pass, because you did not believe my words, which will be fulfilled at their proper time.”
Meanwhile, the people were waiting for Zechariah and wondering why he took so long in the temple.
When he came out and was unable to speak to them, they realized he had seen a vision in the temple. He kept making signs to them but remained unable to speak.
And when the days of his service were complete, he returned home.

units.label: Elizabeth's hidden joy
units.note: the Lord has taken away my disgrace

verses.text: After these days, his wife Elizabeth became pregnant and for five months remained in seclusion. She declared,
“The Lord has done this for me. In these days He has shown me favor and taken away my disgrace among the people.”

kathexes.gloss: In order, in sequence, one after another. Luke says he will write an orderly account. He is not claiming a bare chronology so much as a coherent, well-arranged telling that lets the reader see how the pieces fit and where the story is going, from the temple to the empty tomb.

asphaleia.gloss: Certainty, security, firm and unshakable footing. It is the last word of the preface in the Greek and the goal of the whole Gospel: Theophilus, and every reader, may know that the things he has been taught are solid ground, not rumor.

pneuma.gloss: Spirit, breath, wind. Here the Holy Spirit, who fills John even in the womb. Luke's Gospel and its sequel, Acts, are marked by the Spirit at every turn, coming upon the humble to prepare and empower them for God's work.

euangelizo.gloss: To announce good news, to proclaim glad tidings. Gabriel brings good news to Zechariah, and the same word runs through Luke: to the shepherds, to the poor, and out to the nations. The Gospel of joy begins with an angel's announcement in the temple.

basic.context: Luke begins with a short preface, unusual among the Gospels, telling us he has carefully investigated everything and is writing an orderly account for a man named Theophilus so that he may be sure of what he has been taught. Then the story proper opens in the temple. Zechariah, an old priest, and his wife Elizabeth are righteous but childless, a grief in their world. While Zechariah offers incense, the angel Gabriel tells him his prayer is answered: a son, John, will be born who will go before the Lord in the spirit of Elijah. Zechariah doubts, and is struck silent until it comes true. Elizabeth conceives and rejoices that God has taken away her disgrace.
basic.hermeneutics: The first four verses tell us how to read the whole book: as a careful, truthful account meant to give assurance, not a collection of legends. From verse 5 the narrative unfolds in the style of Old Testament birth stories, so that we hear echoes of Abraham and Sarah, of Hannah, of the barren who are given a child by God. The key questions are simple: why does Luke write, and what is God doing when He breaks four centuries of prophetic silence to answer an old couple's prayer?
basic.originalLanguages: Luke promises an orderly account, kathexes, so that Theophilus may know the certainty, asphaleia, of what he has learned. Gabriel brings good news, the verb euangelizo that will echo through the Gospel, and the child will be filled with the pneuma, the Holy Spirit, from the womb.
basic.typology: John comes in the spirit and power of Elijah, fulfilling the promise that Elijah would come before the great day of the Lord. The barren womb opened by God recalls Sarah, Rebekah, and Hannah, and points to the God who gives life where there is none, ultimately at the virgin's womb and the empty tomb.
basic.christ: This chapter is all preparation for Christ. John is not the light but the one who goes before the Lord to make ready a people for Him. Every promise here leans forward to Jesus, the Lord whose way John will prepare.
basic.god: God is the one who keeps His word across long silence, who hears prayer, who remembers the humble and the barren, and who works through ordinary faithful people like Zechariah and Elizabeth. He is gracious even in the discipline of Zechariah's silence.
basic.crossReferences: Malachi 4 promises Elijah before the day of the Lord. 1 Samuel 1 tells of Hannah, another woman given a son by God. Genesis 18 recounts the promise to aged Abraham and Sarah. And Acts 1 shows Luke's second volume, addressed again to Theophilus.
basic.oneStory: After centuries of waiting, God moves again. He answers prayer, opens a barren womb, and sends the forerunner, so that the whole Gospel can begin with the note that will never leave it: God is keeping His promises, and joy is coming.

context.literary: The passage falls into two clearly marked parts: a formal, polished Greek preface, verses 1 to 4, the most classical sentence in the New Testament, and then the first infancy scene in a deliberately Old Testament style, verses 5 to 25. Luke pairs the births of John and Jesus throughout chapters 1 and 2, and this is the first panel, set in the temple, the very heart of Israel's worship.
context.historical: The scene is set in the reign of Herod the Great, before the census of chapter 2. Priests served in twenty-four divisions, and the honor of burning incense fell by lot, perhaps once in a lifetime, which makes this the great day of Zechariah's ministry. Childlessness was felt as a deep sorrow and sometimes a reproach, so Elizabeth's words carry real weight.
context.canonical: Luke opens the third Gospel and, with Acts, the largest single contribution to the New Testament. The preface signals a careful historian at work. The Elijah promise ties the story to the last words of the Old Testament in Malachi, so that the New Testament begins by picking up exactly where the Old left off, waiting for the Lord to come.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To give an ordered, well-attested account of the things fulfilled among believers, grounding the reader in certainty, and to open the story by showing God faithfully beginning to fulfill His ancient promises through the birth of the forerunner.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes what God did in a particular time and place. It does not promise every believer a child or an angelic visit. What abides is the trustworthiness of the gospel record, God's faithfulness to His word, and His readiness to hear prayer and honor humble, ordinary obedience.

debates.question: Who is Theophilus, and why does Luke address him?

views.label: A real individual
views.case: The name means friend or lover of God, and most read him as a real person, perhaps a patron or a recent convert, addressed with the honorific most excellent as one would address an official.

views.label: A representative reader
views.case: Some suggest the name stands for any God-loving reader; even so, Luke plainly writes to give a named reader firm assurance, and every later reader is drawn into that same purpose.

debates.bearing: Either way, the point of the preface stands: Luke writes verifiable history so that the reader may be certain of the gospel he has received.

debates.question: Why was Zechariah struck silent when Mary, who also questioned, was not?

views.label: A discipline for unbelief
views.case: Gabriel says plainly it is because he did not believe; a priest who knew the Scriptures and the stories of Abraham and Hannah asked for a sign in doubt rather than trusting the word.

views.label: A merciful sign, not mere punishment
views.case: The silence is also the very sign he asked for, and it becomes the setting for his later song of praise; discipline and gift are woven together.

debates.bearing: The contrast with Mary's humble how can this be invites the reader to receive God's promises with trust rather than demand for proof.

study.originalLanguages: The preface is a single, carefully built sentence promising an orderly account, kathexes, so that Theophilus may know the asphaleia, the certainty, of what he has been taught, the firm ground beneath his faith. When Gabriel appears he brings good news, euangelizo, the verb of the gospel that will sound again to the shepherds and to the nations. John will be filled with the pneuma, the Holy Spirit, from his mother's womb, the first of the Spirit's many comings in a Gospel soaked in the Spirit's work. So the vocabulary of the opening already announces Luke's themes: certainty, good news, and the Spirit who prepares a people for the Lord.

typology.type: The barren womb opened by God
typology.fulfillment: Like Sarah and Hannah, Elizabeth conceives by God's gift, a pattern that reaches its height when the virgin conceives and the tomb gives back its dead, 1 Samuel 1 and Luke 1:37.

typology.type: John in the spirit and power of Elijah
typology.fulfillment: The promised return of Elijah before the day of the Lord, fulfilled in John who prepares the way for Christ, Malachi 4:5-6 and Luke 7:27.
The herald who readies Israel for the coming King, fulfilled as John points to Jesus, the Lamb of God, John 1:29-31.

study.christ: Christ is not yet on the scene, but the whole passage bends toward Him. John will go on before the Lord to make ready a people prepared for the Lord; he is the voice, not the Word, the friend of the Bridegroom, not the Bridegroom. The Lord whose way John prepares is the Lord Jesus, and the joy promised at John's birth is only the overture to the greater joy of the Savior's coming. Even here, the God who answers prayer and opens the barren womb is the God who will do the greater wonder of the incarnation, and John's task is simply to point away from himself to the One who comes after him.
study.god: The God of this chapter keeps His promises across four centuries of prophetic silence. He hears the prayers of an old, disappointed couple and remembers the humble. He is faithful to His covenant, sending the forerunner exactly as Malachi foretold. He is gracious even in discipline, for Zechariah's silence is both a rebuke and a gift. And He delights to work through unglamorous, faithful obedience, a priest at his duty, a woman in her seclusion, so that the great story of salvation opens not with the powerful of Rome but with the prayers of the temple.

crossReferences.note: The promise that God would send Elijah to turn the hearts of fathers and children before the day of the Lord, fulfilled in John.
Hannah, barren and grieved, is given a son by God, an Old Testament pattern behind Elizabeth's story.
God's promise of a son to aged Abraham and Sarah; nothing is too hard for the Lord.
Luke's second volume, again addressed to Theophilus, showing the two-part orderly account.

study.oneStory: The long night of waiting since the last prophet begins to lift. God answers prayer, opens a barren womb, and raises up the forerunner promised by Malachi. Everything in this movement leans forward: John is sent to make ready a people for the Lord, and the Lord is Jesus. So the Gospel opens with the quiet certainty that God is faithful, and the story that runs all the way to the cross, the empty tomb, and the ends of the earth has begun.

sermon.title: Elizabeth and Zechariah
sermon.why: From Begg's series on Luke's infancy narratives, this message expounds Luke 1:5-23, the account of the aged, childless priest and his wife and the promise of John, showing how God works amazing things in the midst of ordinary, faithful lives. A careful, expository handling of this movement.

quiz.q: Why does Luke say he wrote his Gospel?

options.text: So that Theophilus may know the certainty of the things he has been taught.
options.note: Luke states this purpose plainly in verse 4: assurance grounded in a careful account.

options.text: To give an orderly, carefully investigated account of what was fulfilled.
options.note: True, and Luke says this, but the account serves the deeper goal of the reader's certainty.

options.text: To record his own personal opinions about Jesus.
options.note: Luke relies on eyewitnesses and careful investigation, not mere opinion.

options.text: To entertain readers with religious legends.
options.note: Luke writes verifiable history for assurance, the opposite of legend.

quiz.q: What did the angel Gabriel tell Zechariah?

options.text: His prayer was heard, and Elizabeth would bear a son, John, who would go before the Lord in the spirit of Elijah.
options.note: This is the heart of Gabriel's message in verses 13 to 17.

options.text: Elizabeth would have a son who would bring him great joy.
options.note: True, but it leaves out John's central mission to prepare a people for the Lord.

options.text: Zechariah himself would become a great prophet.
options.note: The promise concerned his son John, not Zechariah's own prophetic career.

options.text: He should stop praying because God would not answer.
options.note: The opposite: God had heard and was answering his prayer.

quiz.q: Why was Zechariah unable to speak?

options.text: Because he did not believe Gabriel's words, so he was silent until they were fulfilled.
options.note: Gabriel gives this reason directly in verse 20.

options.text: Because he had seen a vision in the temple and was overwhelmed.
options.note: The people guessed this, but the stated cause was his unbelief.

options.text: Because it was the custom for priests to keep silence after serving.
options.note: There was no such custom; the silence was a specific sign to Zechariah.

options.text: Because Elizabeth had asked him not to tell anyone.
options.note: The silence came from Gabriel's word, not from Elizabeth.

quiz.q: In whose spirit and power would John come?

options.text: In the spirit and power of Elijah, to turn hearts and make ready a people for the Lord.
options.note: Verse 17 names Elijah, fulfilling Malachi's promise.

options.text: In the power of the Holy Spirit, with whom he was filled from the womb.
options.note: True and stated in verse 15, but the specific comparison in verse 17 is to Elijah.

options.text: In the spirit of Moses the lawgiver.
options.note: The text points to Elijah, the promised forerunner, not Moses.

options.text: In his own strength and eloquence.
options.note: John's power was from God, not himself.

quiz.q: How did Elizabeth respond when she conceived?

options.text: She rejoiced that the Lord had shown her favor and taken away her disgrace.
options.note: Her words in verse 25 give thanks to God for removing her reproach.

options.text: She kept to herself in seclusion for five months.
options.note: True, she did remain in seclusion, but her heart's response was thankful joy in God.

options.text: She doubted the pregnancy could be real.
options.note: It was Zechariah who doubted; Elizabeth praised God.

options.text: She complained that it had come too late in life.
options.note: She gave thanks, not complaint, for God's kindness.

questions: Luke wrote so that his reader might know the certainty of the gospel. Where do you long for more assurance, and how might a careful look at the Scriptures steady your faith?
God broke centuries of silence to answer the prayers of an ordinary couple. Have you grown weary in a prayer you have prayed for years, and what would it mean to keep bringing it to God?
Zechariah and Elizabeth were righteous, yet they still carried a deep disappointment. How do you hold together faithfulness to God and the ache of unanswered longing?
Zechariah doubted and was disciplined, yet God still kept His promise. How does it comfort you that God's purposes do not depend on the strength of your faith?
John's whole calling was to point away from himself to the Lord. In what ways is God calling you to prepare the way for others to see Jesus?

translationNotes.note: The BSB source places an em dash before to make ready a people prepared for the Lord, following the sentence structure that quotes Malachi 4:5-6. In this edition the dash is rendered as a comma so the clause reads smoothly, without changing the sense.
Orderly account renders a Greek word meaning in sequence or in an arranged order; Luke claims a coherent, well-ordered telling, not merely a strict calendar of events.
Filled with the Holy Spirit even from his mother's womb marks John as set apart by God before birth, echoing the language of Nazirite dedication and the calling of prophets like Samuel and Jeremiah.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Luke 1:1-25. Heading, line break, note, and poem markers were skipped when assembling verse text. The em dash in Luke 1:17 was converted to a comma; no em or en dashes remain.
